metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the caused_by auto-link vs. closed-source guard conflict: specify whether (a) auto-link calls `_write_link_event` directly (bypassing `add_dependency`'s closed-source guard), (b) `caused_by` is explicitly exempted from the closed-source guard in `add_dependency`, or (c) blame resolution runs before `transition_core` commits — and document the chosen ordering in ticket 555e's Approach.
evidence: 555e: 'on bug close, best-effort auto-link caused_by' — the auto-link must be called after the fixing commit exists (blame needs it), i.e., after transition_core commits
_links.py:242-247: 'source_status = _get_ticket_status(source_id, tracker_dir); if source_status == "closed": raise ValueError(...)' — this guard fires for ALL relations, not just blocking ones
transition_close.close_ticket ordering: _completion_precheck → txn.transition_core (STATUS committed, bug now closed) → signing → compact-on-close
ABSENCE: no plan text addresses this ordering conflict or explains how caused_by auto-link avoids the closed-source guard
findings.finding: The caused_by auto-link fires after transition_core commits the STATUS event (to access the fixing commit for blame), but add_dependency in graph/_links.py:242-247 unconditionally rejects writes where the source ticket is already closed — so every auto-derived caused_by link will silently fail (best-effort swallows the ValueError), leaving the feature a no-op for auto-derivation.
findings.location: Approach section ('blame-based auto-derivation'), ticket 555e (transition_close.py auto-link), src/rebar/graph/_links.py lines 242-247
scenarios: Agent closes bug abc1 with --class regression; blame resolves a single culprit; auto-link calls add_dependency(abc1, culprit, 'caused_by') after transition_core; add_dependency reads abc1 as closed and raises ValueError; best-effort wrapper swallows it; no caused_by edge is written — the provenance metric never populates from auto-derivation
All auto-derived caused_by edges silently fail; only explicit --caused-by <id> overrides work, making the auto-derivation design a dead letter
findings.suggested_fix: Exempt non-blocking relations (relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from, and the new caused_by) from the closed-source guard in add_dependency, since these relations carry no scheduling semantics and a closed ticket legitimately receives them as observability links. Or, have 555e's auto-link call _write_link_event directly (the lower-level writer that bypasses the guard), documenting why the cycle/closed checks are not needed for a non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing relation on a closed source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` is not gitignored (`.gitignore` currently ignores `/reports/` at line 74), and either move the snapshot store to a non-ignored path (e.g. `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` or `data/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`) or add an explicit `!reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` exception and document the commit strategy.
evidence: ticket 3275 description: 'reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson — new committed artifact (one snapshot per line), mirroring the existing reports/*.report.json precedent'
.gitignore line 74: '/reports/'
scripts/run_live_matrix.py lines 6-7: derives the JSON summary at 'reports/d01e-live-matrix.report.json' — a CI artifact, not a committed file
docs/adr/0037: report.json is a CI-generated artifact in reports/, not tracked in git
findings.finding: The plan (ticket 3275) asserts that `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` will be a 'committed artifact' mirroring 'the existing `reports/*.report.json` precedent', but `.gitignore` line 74 shows `/reports/` is gitignored at the repo root. The only `reports/` files referenced in docs (e.g. `d01e-live-matrix.report.json` in ADR-0037) are CI output artifacts that are never committed. If `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` lives under `/reports/`, git will ignore it, no snapshots will ever accumulate in the repo, and the going-forward trend capability will silently not work.
findings.location: Decomposition / Backfill track; ticket 3275 (Going-forward metrics snapshot writer/reader), Files section: '`reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` — new committed artifact (one snapshot per line), mirroring the existing `reports/*.report.json` precedent'
scenarios: An implementer writes `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` as directed; git ignores the file; `rebar metrics` never accumulates snapshot history; every code-health metric reports `unavailable` forever.
A CI run appends a snapshot, it is never committed, and the metric series resets on every fresh checkout.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the snapshot store to `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` (already tracked as part of the rebar store) or `data/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`, and update ticket 3275's file_impact and the Backfill track accordingly. Alternatively, add `!reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` to `.gitignore` if keeping the `reports/` location, with an explicit CI step that commits the file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Change 5062's backfill label to `source=backfill_classified` to match the parent's single backfill vocabulary, and update 5062's AC to assert `source=backfill_classified`.
evidence: Parent SC5: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`)'
Parent AC3: '`is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False'
5062 Approach: 'Backfilled classes carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
c085 defines is_authoritative() to exclude backfill_classified — backfill_structural would pass through as authoritative
findings.finding: Child 5062 labels backfilled close_class records `source=backfill_structural` but the parent plan explicitly states there is no `backfill_structural` and defines a single backfill vocabulary of `source=backfill_classified`. This contradicts the parent's SC5 and AC3/AC5, and means 5062's output may be treated as authoritative by c085's `is_authoritative()` helper (which only excludes `backfill_classified`), polluting the authoritative series.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61, Approach section: 'Backfilled classes carry `source=backfill_structural, confidence=classified`'
scenarios: Bug-class backfill runs; 5062 writes source=backfill_structural; c085's is_authoritative() returns True for it; the authoritative aggregation includes backfilled classification data, violating the segregation guarantee
Parent AC5 integration test checks for source=backfill_classified but 5062 writes backfill_structural — the test fails
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py` and `tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_bug_class.py`, replace every occurrence of `source=backfill_structural` with `source=backfill_classified` to match the parent-mandated single vocabulary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Label c595 backfill snapshots `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (or introduce a distinct `source=backfill_snapshot` and make is_authoritative() return False for it), distinguishing seeded history from live going-forward snapshots.
evidence: Parent SC5: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified`'
c595 Approach: 'Writes through the same snapshot.py writer as the going-forward path... labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'
c085 defines authoritative sources as {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot} — so snapshot is authoritative
c595 is an explicit backfill script for historical data that did not exist before
findings.finding: Child c595 labels backfill complexity/clone snapshots `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, but these are historical backfilled values and the parent requires ALL backfilled values carry `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`. Using `source=snapshot` causes them to be treated as authoritative by c085's `is_authoritative()`, violating the parent's segregation guarantee for backfill data.
findings.location: child c595-9348-d4f2-4529, Approach section: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'
scenarios: c595 backfill runs; snapshots are written with source=snapshot; c085's is_authoritative() returns True; the complexity/clone trend series mixes deterministic-but-historical backfill with live going-forward snapshots without any label distinguishing them
A downstream consumer wanting to audit how far back the series is trustworthy cannot identify the seeded portion
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`, write snapshots with `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified` (matching the parent's single backfill vocabulary). Update c595's AC to assert those labels, and update c085's is_authoritative() to exclude backfill_classified consistently.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `accruing_since` population rule for `Unavailable` (e.g., 'the wall-clock ISO date when `rebar metrics` is first run after epic completion, or the tracker init date, or a per-MetricSpec declared anchor') in the registry contract (c085 or the epic Approach section) so all MetricSpec implementors use the same value.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured `unavailable` state and light up automatically as data arrives, with no future code change'
c085 AC2: 'A metric with no data returns `Unavailable` carrying a non-empty `reason` and an ISO `accruing_since`'
ABSENCE: no rule anywhere in the plan or child tickets specifies how `accruing_since` is determined for a metric with no data yet
findings.finding: The `unavailable` state's `accruing_since` field has no specified population rule. The plan and child ticket c085 say the field must be 'an ISO `accruing_since`' (c085 AC2) and 'light up automatically as data arrives' (epic Success Criteria), but never specifies what timestamp value to write: the epic creation date, the first event in the store, the current wall-clock time, or some metric-specific anchor. This is a processing protocol referenced but not co-located with the schema.
findings.location: Success Criteria ('metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured `unavailable` state'); child ticket c085 AC2 ('a non-empty `reason` and an ISO `accruing_since`')
scenarios: Two implementors of MetricSpec.compute() will write different `accruing_since` values (today's date vs tracker init date vs first event), causing the no-missing-key integration test to pass but the field to be semantically inconsistent across metrics.
The 'light up automatically' claim requires that `accruing_since` is meaningful, but without a rule it could be the far future (breaking the light-up behavior).
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the registry contract: '`accruing_since` is the ISO date of the tracker's earliest event (from `.env-id` creation timestamp), giving each metric a consistent 'data starts here' anchor that is the same for all structural readers and 0001-01-01 for a brand-new store.'

findings.finding: Child 5062 emits `source=backfill_structural` but child c085's authoritative source vocabulary enumerates only `{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}`. The value `backfill_structural` is absent from c085's enum, so `is_authoritative('backfill_structural')` will fall into an undefined/default branch — likely returning False (correct intent) but without an explicit guard, making the segregation guarantee fragile and the schema inconsistent.
findings.location: child 5062-30a8-9153-4c61 (Approach section) vs child c085-6d11-d68d-422a (Approach section)
findings.suggested_fix: In c085's registry.py, add `backfill_structural` to the source enum and ensure `is_authoritative('backfill_structural')` returns False explicitly. Update 5062's test to assert the label using the c085-canonical value.

evidence: docs/ticket-model.md: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py: 555e-3efa-4ad6-4169 & ed13-e224-1ece-44ff (no ordering edge)
tests/golden/cli_help/__overview_help.out: 0b96-cbcb-0ea2-479d & 9a5a-d9ea-47c1-4e6a (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify what happens to existing callers that pass `--reason <value>` after the swap to `--class`: document whether `--reason` becomes an unknown-argument error (with a migration hint in the error message), a deprecated alias, or is simply removed — and add this to the ed13 AC.
evidence: 'the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none' — this describes the interface contract but not the fate of callers passing the OLD arg
'its error message must enumerate the allowed values so any existing caller that omitted it fails loudly' — only the omit-case is covered, not the pass-old-arg case
ABSENCE: no mention of `--reason` deprecation path, unknown-argument error, or migration advisory for callers currently passing `--reason <value>`
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges callers that *omit* `--reason`/`--class` (they get a loud error with allowed values), but does not address what happens to existing scripts that currently *pass* `--reason <value>` — whether `--reason` becomes an unknown/rejected arg, is silently dropped, or is deprecated with a migration message is unspecified.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'; AC 'Bug-close friction stays flat'
scenarios: A CI script or agent wrapper currently calls `rebar transition BUG-123 in_progress closed --reason 'fixed the bug'`; after the change, this either silently drops `--reason` (wrong) or raises an unguided unknown-argument error — neither is handled by the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ed13 section, add: 'If `--reason` is supplied after the change, the CLI raises an `UnknownArgument` error whose message says: `--reason has been replaced by --class; allowed values are: <enum list>`. This is tested by a negative test asserting the old invocation fails with that message.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an actionable error (or a brief deprecation-accept-and-warn window) for callers that pass the old `--reason` flag after ed13 lands, so they receive guidance to switch to `--class` rather than a generic argparse failure.
evidence: Plan says 'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field' — addresses only code/data rollback, not CLI caller migration.

'its error message must enumerate the allowed values so any existing caller that omitted it fails loudly with an actionable message' — scoped to callers omitting --class, not callers supplying --reason.
No mention of a deprecation window, an 'ignored-with-warning' phase for --reason, or an actionable error for callers passing --reason.
findings.finding: The 'back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change' section addresses data compat and code-level rollback, but does not address CLI-level migration for existing callers who currently pass `--reason`. After ed13 lands, a caller that issues `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason <text>` will receive a generic argparse 'unrecognized arguments' error with no actionable guidance — the plan only ensures callers who omit `--class` get an actionable message, not callers who pass the old flag.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'
scenarios: An agent harness, CI script, or test fixture that currently calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason 'some text'` begins failing with a cryptic argparse error immediately after ed13 lands, with no indication of the replacement flag.
During rollback ('ceasing to require/write the field'), callers that have already been updated to pass --class would also break if --class is removed, requiring a second coordinated change.
findings.suggested_fix: In the ed13 implementation of `transition_close.py`, explicitly parse `--reason` as a now-deprecated argument and emit a clear error: 'The --reason argument has been replaced by --class {ALLOWED_VALUES}; please update your invocation.' This requires one extra argparse entry but gives all existing callers an actionable migration message and is trivially removed in a rollback.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to 2f8e asserting that each emitted caused_by link record carries `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified`.
evidence: Parent AC5: 'at least one backfilled `caused_by`/`close_class`/snapshot value exists carrying `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`, asserted by a test over the backfill output'
2f8e AC1: 'the script links the single-culprit bug and skips the ambiguous one (assert links == 1)' — asserts count only
2f8e AC2: 'test asserts the second run draws 0 new links' — asserts idempotency only
No 2f8e AC checks source= or confidence= on the emitted link event
findings.finding: Child 2f8e's Acceptance Criteria do not assert that backfilled `caused_by` links carry `source` and `confidence` labels, leaving the parent's AC5 requirement ('at least one backfilled caused_by value carrying `source=backfill_classified + confidence=classified`, asserted by a test') unverified for the 2f8e backfill path.
findings.location: child 2f8e-0f7e-6fea-4825, Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: 2f8e runs and writes caused_by links without source/confidence labels; parent AC5 integration test fails because no labeled backfill value exists from 2f8e; 538c might provide the only labeled value, leaving parent AC5 contingent on an optional adapter
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/unit/scripts/test_backfill_caused_by.py`, add an assertion: `assert link_record['source'] == 'backfill_classified'` and `assert link_record['confidence'] == 'classified'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate all existing callers of `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason` (agent prompts, CI scripts, golden tests, docs) and either update them in ed13 or add a deprecation warning that redirects to --class.
evidence: ed13 replaces --reason with --class on bug close; the old --reason callers are not enumerated
No child plans a deprecation warning for --reason on bug-close
No child enumerates existing callers of `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason` (agent prompts, CI scripts, docs, tests in other children)
Parent's coordination note says '555e/2f8e tests that close a bug MUST pass --class' — acknowledges the breaking change for child tests but not for external callers
Parent AC4 says 'change swaps one required arg for another' but no consumer enumeration is done
findings.finding: No child addresses the consumer-impact of removing `--reason` from the bug-close path on existing callers. Scripts, documentation, or agent prompts that invoke `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason <text>` will silently break (exit nonzero) after ed13 lands, with no deprecation warning, migration guide, or detection test in any child.
findings.location: child ed13-e224-1ece-44ff; parent Approach: 'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field'
scenarios: An existing CI script or agent prompt calls `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason 'fixed'`; after ed13, it exits nonzero with an error about missing --class; the failure is confusing because --reason was valid before
Documentation examples in docs/user-guide.md still show --reason syntax after ed13; agent trained on that doc uses the wrong flag
findings.suggested_fix: In ed13, add a check in transition.py: if `--reason` is provided on a bug-close path, emit a clear error 'Use --class instead of --reason for bug closes (allowed values: ...)' rather than silently ignoring it or failing with a generic error.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a metric-registry manifest (or a table in the plan) enumerating every expected metric key per lens (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health) so AC #2's 'every registered metric key' has a fixed, checkable reference.
evidence: 'The command is built once against a complete metric registry'
'every registered metric key is present with either a value or {"unavailable"...}'
No section enumerates which metrics belong in the registry (agent-process / gate-economics / code-health are named as lenses but individual metric keys are absent)
findings.finding: The metric registry's contents are never enumerated; the plan says 'built once against a complete metric registry' but leaves the actual set of metric keys to be decided by the executor. This means AC #2 ('every registered metric key is present') passes trivially if the registry is under-populated — and the executor cannot know whether their registry is 'complete'.
findings.location: Success Criteria (metric-registry bullet); Acceptance Criteria line 2; Decomposition (Command track: 'metric-registry + unavailable/label contract')
scenarios: Executor registers only 3 trivial metrics, AC #2 passes, but the real goal (surfacing agent-loop trends) is unmet.
Two child-ticket authors register different subsets with no canonical list to check against, leaving gaps undiscovered.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section 'Metric registry (normative list)' with a table: metric_key | lens | derivation_source | notes. Child tickets register against this list; the capstone integration test imports the manifest and asserts key-for-key coverage.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the snapshot write trigger (e.g., 'written automatically at the end of each CI gate run' or 'written on first `rebar metrics` invocation for the current date') so the executor has a concrete contract.
evidence: 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader' listed without trigger, frequency, or invocation contract
No AC or Success Criterion references snapshot write semantics
findings.finding: The 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader' is listed as a first-class deliverable in the Command track but the plan never specifies when or how snapshots are written — on each `rebar metrics` invocation, on a cron schedule, triggered by a gate event, or via an explicit subcommand. This is a deferred design decision.
findings.location: Decomposition — Command track: 'going-forward snapshot writer/reader'
scenarios: Executor implements on-demand write; another child ticket assumes cron; tests diverge.
Snapshot writer is never triggered in the test harness so metrics that depend on snapshots always report 'unavailable'.
findings.suggested_fix: State the default: 'Snapshot writer runs as a post-step in the gate harness (same invocation point as the gate sidecar write); `rebar metrics` reads but never writes snapshots.' This keeps the command read-only and the write path deterministic.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the pre-epic `rebar transition --help` output as a committed golden file (e.g., `tests/golden/cli_help/transition_help_pre_epic.out`) or specify the exact git ref to diff against, so AC #4 is in-session verifiable.
evidence: 'verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline (the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none)'
No golden file path or git ref is named for the pre-epic state
findings.finding: AC #4's help-diff check references 'the pre-epic baseline' for `rebar transition --help` but that baseline is not captured in the plan (no golden file path, no git ref, no snapshot). An executor cannot run this check in-session without first locating or reconstructing the baseline.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 4: 'verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline'
scenarios: Executor cannot find the baseline and skips the diff check, leaving the friction-flat guarantee unverified.
Executor diffs against the wrong ref (e.g., current HEAD before their change) and the check passes vacuously.
findings.suggested_fix: Commit the current `rebar transition --help` output to `tests/golden/cli_help/transition_help_baseline.out` as part of the first child ticket (ed13 pre-work); the AC then diffs the post-ed13 output against that file and asserts the required-arg count is unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a statement in Success Criteria or Approach clarifying that the `ERROR` gate-sidecar outcome serves as the *start* anchor and the subsequent clean gate serves as the *end* anchor, and that the metrics command derives the diagnosis *interval* from this pair — so the solution description matches the problem (intervals, not just outcomes).
evidence: Context: 'env/integration-diagnosis intervals (an infra failure starts a diagnosis, its next clean gate ends it)'
Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Approach section never describes how the metrics command derives a duration from the ERROR event + next-clean-gate pair
No AC verifies that a diagnosis *duration* is computed, only that the metrics artifact spans 'agent-process' metrics
findings.finding: The Context defines Tier-C blindspot (a) as capturing *intervals* (diagnosis duration from failure start to next clean gate), but the Success Criteria only describes adding an `ERROR` outcome to existing gate sidecars — a single event, not an interval pair. The derivation logic mapping ERROR-event → interval computation is never stated in the plan body, creating a contradiction between the problem description (intervals are the blindspot) and the solution description (an ERROR outcome closes the blindspot).
findings.location: Context (Tier-C blindspots definition) vs Success Criteria (third bullet: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars')
scenarios: Implementer reads Success Criteria and emits ERROR outcomes on gate sidecars without implementing the start→end interval derivation in the metrics command, satisfying the SC literally while the actual blindspot (diagnosis duration) remains uncomputed.
Reviewer accepts the ERROR-outcome instrumentation as complete without noticing that no reader derives the interval.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria third bullet, extend to: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars (the ERROR event anchors the start of a diagnosis interval; the reader derives the duration to the subsequent clean gate for the same scope, emitting it as a metric).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the metric-registry schema ('unavailable'/'accruing_since' contract), the 'is_authoritative()' / confidence-vocabulary rules, and the ERROR-outcome-on-gate-sidecar pattern in an ADR or AGENTS.md section so child-ticket authors have a single authoritative reference.
evidence: 'The command is built once against a complete metric registry: metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured unavailable state and light up automatically as data arrives, with no future code change.'
'is_authoritative() returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series'
'the gate sidecar reader + ERROR path' (new passive instrumentation pattern reusing existing sidecar surface)
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan introduces multiple novel architectural decisions — the metric-registry 'unavailable'/'accruing_since' contract, the 'is_authoritative()' / 'backfill_classified' confidence vocabulary, the ERROR-outcome-on-gate-sidecar passive instrumentation pattern, and the caused_by link-relation type — but calls for no ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry to capture any of them. These patterns must be discoverable by future contributors and child-ticket authors who will register new metrics or read the confidence contract.
findings.location: Approach section; Success Criteria ('metric registry', 'is_authoritative()', 'source=backfill_classified'); Decomposition ('append-only registration seam')
scenarios: A contributor adding a new metric in a child ticket must discover the is_authoritative() contract and unavailable schema from the epic prose alone — no canonical design artifact exists; a misimplemented reader that omits confidence labeling would silently contaminate authoritative aggregations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or an `## Architecture` section in AGENTS.md) that formally defines: (1) the MetricEntry schema including the unavailable sentinel structure, (2) the allowed confidence/source values and when is_authoritative() returns True vs False, (3) the ERROR-outcome convention on gate sidecars, and (4) the caused_by link-relation schema. Reference this artifact from each child ticket.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace bare `jq -e` shell assertions in the integration-test ACs with a Python-native JSON assertion (e.g., `python -m json.tool` + key-presence check, or a pytest fixture) so the tests pass on Windows without requiring jq to be pre-installed.
evidence: 'an integration test asserts via `jq -e` that no registered metric key is absent (the capstone 9a5a AC asserts this exact no-missing-key check, not merely a non-empty map)'
'rebar show <closed-bug> --output json | jq -e \'.close_class\''

jq is not part of the Windows standard toolchain; it must be explicitly installed (e.g., via Chocolatey or winget) and is absent from stock Windows CI images such as windows-latest on GitHub Actions unless separately provisioned.
findings.finding: Two acceptance criteria use `jq` as a shell command to assert on JSON output ('an integration test asserts via `jq -e` that no registered metric key is absent'; '`rebar show <closed-bug> --output json | jq -e '.close_class'`'). `jq` is not bundled with Windows and is not on the standard Windows PATH, so these integration-test assertions will fail on a Windows platform CI runner even when the rebar command under test behaves correctly.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 2 ('integration test asserts via `jq -e`'); Acceptance Criteria bullet 4 ('rebar show <closed-bug> --output json | jq -e '.close_class'')
scenarios: Windows platform CI runner (e.g., GitHub Actions windows-latest): the integration test shell command `jq -e` exits with 'jq: command not found' (or equivalent), causing the test to fail with a missing-binary error rather than a meaningful assertion failure, masking whether rebar metrics itself is correct.
findings.suggested_fix: In the integration test for 9a5a, parse the JSON output with Python's stdlib `json` module and assert key presence; for the bug-close AC, use `rebar show --output json` piped into a Python one-liner (`python -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); assert 'close_class' in d"`) or a pytest parametrized fixture, eliminating the jq dependency.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether snowwhite-mysterious-macropod (metrics CLI) should land earlier as a thin walking skeleton — registry + CLI + one reader returning mostly `unavailable` — to validate the no-missing-key contract and JSON schema before all readers are stacked on top, exploiting the unavailable-state design that already exists for this purpose.
evidence: 'The command is built once against a complete metric registry: metrics whose signals have not yet accrued report a structured unavailable state and light up automatically as data arrives, with no future code change.'

CLI child snowwhite-mysterious-macropod is listed last in the Command track, implying it lands after illadvised-sophomoric-jellyfish (registry), bonelike-facile-inganue (event readers), exciting-chargeable-monarch (git readers), paralyzed-sane-flyingfox (gate-sidecar reader), and cyberpunk-spineless-bear (snapshot writer/reader).

The capstone AC ('no registered metric key is absent') can only be validated once all readers are registered, but a partial integration test (registry + CLI + one reader, rest unavailable) could validate the no-missing-key invariant much earlier.
Cross-ticket coordination calls out three specific collision points but does not address the sequencing of CLI vs. readers.
findings.finding: The CLI child (snowwhite-mysterious-macropod) is sequenced as a capstone after all readers land, but the unavailable-state contract is specifically designed to allow the CLI to render meaningful output (all metrics 'unavailable') before any readers exist — an earlier thin walking skeleton (registry + CLI + one reader) could de-risk the integration earlier and validate the no-missing-key contract before the full reader set is built.
findings.location: Decomposition section — three tracks listed with CLI as capstone; Cross-ticket coordination section
scenarios: If the registry/unavailable contract has a design flaw (e.g., keys registered late or unavailable rendering broken), this is only discovered at the capstone integration test after all reader children are complete — expensive rework.
If the CLI's JSON schema or jq assertion format is wrong, fixing it requires touching the capstone child after all readers are already stacked on top.
findings.suggested_fix: Reorder the Command track so that illadvised-sophomoric-jellyfish (registry) → snowwhite-mysterious-macropod (CLI, thin — renders all unavailable) → readers land sequentially. This validates the pipeline shape early and lets the capstone AC accumulate incrementally rather than being tested only at the end.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the epic plan (and the child ticket covering the 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path') that closing the env/integration-diagnosis blindspot requires ADDING a new ERROR-outcome sidecar emit inside the gate exception handlers (transition_close.py and plan-review error paths), not merely reading from an existing one — and name the specific files and call sites.
evidence: Plan: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Plan Approach: 'We reuse existing surfaces (gate sidecars, the transition command, the link-relation set) rather than introduce a new event type'
transition_close.py lines 188-211: the except block calls _failure.outcome_of(exc), logs, then raises CommandError — no sidecar emit on the error path
completion_sidecar.emit() is called at line 240 (FAIL path) and line 254 (PASS path) — no call exists in the exception handler at line 188
REVIEW_RESULT sidecar (plan_review/sidecar.py): emitted only by the plan-review gate on PASS/BLOCK, never on error/outage
findings.finding: The plan describes capturing env/integration-diagnosis intervals via an 'ERROR outcome on the existing gate sidecars,' framing this as reusing existing surfaces — but the REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars currently have NO error-path write: when the LLM verifier raises (network outage, 429, etc.), transition_close.py raises CommandError without emitting any sidecar event. Implementing this requires adding new write logic to the gate error handlers, which the plan does not specify.
findings.location: Tier-C blindspots definition, Approach section ('reuse existing surfaces (gate sidecars)'), src/rebar/_commands/transition_close.py lines 188-211
scenarios: Gate hits a network outage (429/503); the error handler raises CommandError; no sidecar is written; the diagnosis interval is unrecorded; the metrics reader finds no ERROR events to bound the interval
An implementer reads 'reuse existing surfaces' and searches for an existing ERROR emit path to read from — finds none — and does not know they must add a new emit to the error handler

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assert the post-rollback schema state in ed13: state that already-written close_class STATUS events remain valid and are intentionally retained (the additive field is safe residue), OR specify a compensating-forward cleanup step (e.g. a one-time EDIT event clearing close_class) if the field must be erased after rollback.
evidence: ed13 Approach: 'back-out = stop requiring/writing it'
Plan Approach: 'back-out is simply ceasing to require/write the field'
No statement about what close_class values already persisted in STATUS events look like after rollback — the reducer will still fold them into reduced state for those tickets
findings.finding: The plan states back-out of the close_class change is 'ceasing to require/write the field', but does not address the post-rollback schema state for STATUS events already written with close_class. Those events remain permanently in the append-only log and the reducer will continue to surface close_class on affected tickets after rollback, which may be unexpected.
findings.location: Approach > 'Back-out / expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change (ed13)'; ticket ed13 Approach section
scenarios: ed13 is landed, several bugs are closed with --class, then the team decides to revert the change. The CLI stops emitting --class, but existing closed bugs show close_class in rebar show output indefinitely, creating a split-brain in the dataset where some bugs have the field and others do not.
A downstream metric or report assumes close_class is absent if the feature was rolled back, but finds it present on pre-rollback tickets.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Create a child ticket for 'historical bug --class backfill' (analogous to 2f8e for caused_by) specifying: the mechanism (structural derivation from old --reason prefixes?), idempotency guard (skip bugs already having close_class), batching strategy, and rollback path — OR explicitly remove this item from scope and document why it is deferred.
evidence: Decomposition: 'Backfill (one-time, labeled): caused_by over existing bugs; historical bug --class from structural signals; complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
No child ticket found covering 'historical bug --class from structural signals' (tickets searched: ed13, 2f8e, 555e, 0b96, 9a5a, 18e6, 3c07, 7931, 3275, 8bc5, c085)
T11(b)(c)(e): batching, resumability, and rollback safety are unspecified for this backfill
findings.finding: The Decomposition lists 'historical bug --class from structural signals' as a backfill item, but no child ticket covers it with safety controls (idempotency, batching, rollback). The only close-class backfill ticket (ed13) adds the forward-going field; the historical classification backfill is undocumented as a spec.
findings.location: Decomposition > Backfill track: 'historical bug --class from structural signals'
scenarios: An implementer discovers the 'historical bug --class' backfill during execution and writes an ad-hoc script with no idempotency guard, double-writing close_class on STATUS events that already had it from the forward-going change.
The backfill emits STATUS EDIT events to inject close_class retroactively — a pattern not established by any existing backfill in the codebase — and the mechanism for doing so is unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either create a child ticket for the complexity/clone snapshot backfill specifying idempotency (deduplicate by commit SHA in the NDJSON before appending), batching (one sampled commit at a time with progress), and rollback (truncate to pre-backfill line count) — OR explicitly remove this item from scope and note it as a future adapter.
evidence: Decomposition: 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits' listed in the Backfill track
Ticket 3275 (snapshot.py) covers the forward-going writer/reader but not a historical commit-walk backfill
No child ticket found covering the sampled-commit backfill
T11(c): 'a partially-completed migration is resumable/idempotent'
findings.finding: The 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits' backfill is listed in Decomposition but has no child ticket with T11 safety controls (idempotency of snapshot writes, resumability if the commit walk is interrupted, rollback if corrupt snapshots are appended to the NDJSON file).
findings.location: Decomposition > Backfill track: 'complexity/clone snapshots at sampled commits'
scenarios: A commit-walk over thousands of git commits to compute complexity/clone at each sampled point is interrupted midway; the NDJSON file now has partial data and re-running from scratch re-appends duplicates (3275 only states malformed-line skipping, not duplicate-snapshot deduplication).
A corrupt complexity value at a sampled commit is appended; the reader skips malformed JSON but a semantically wrong numeric value passes the JSON parser and taints the trend series.
findings.suggested_fix: In the snapshot backfill script, before appending a snapshot, check whether a record with the same `commit_sha` + `metric_id` already exists in `reports/metrics-snapshots.ndjson`; skip if present. Track the last processed commit SHA to a sidecar file for resumability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `git blame` invocation surface in child ticket 555e: confirm `--porcelain` (or `--line-porcelain`) for machine-parseable output, document rename-tracking behavior, and add a flag-level example invocation so implementers cannot choose an incompatible output format.
evidence: Zero hits for 'git blame' and '["git", "blame"' in src/rebar, tests/, scripts/ — this is a new integration surface
Existing git integrations use git log (authorship.py) and git update-ref (_ref_lock.py) with well-specified flag sets
The plan specifies *what* to blame (pre-fix parent state, file_impact paths) but not *how* (blame flags, output format parser, rename handling)
Evidence class: Partially-verified (git subprocess pattern exists; blame-specific surface is unverified)
findings.finding: The `caused_by` blame-based auto-derivation asserts a specific mechanical approach — blaming the *parent* of the fixing commit over the bug's `file_impact` paths — but `git blame` is not used anywhere in the codebase (zero hits in src/rebar, tests/, scripts/), making this a new integration surface. The plan does not specify the exact invocation (e.g., whether --porcelain is used for machine-parseable output, how file renames tracked by -M/-C are handled, how binary/large files are skipped, or timeout bounds), leaving the feasibility of the specific parent-commit blame unverified at the flag level.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Blame-derivation is best-effort and non-authoritative'; Instrumentation track — 'blame-based caused_by auto-derivation'
scenarios: Implementer uses `git blame <sha> -- <file>` and parses the default porcelain-free output, which is human-readable and fragile to parse.

A renamed file in file_impact causes blame to silently return nothing (file not found at parent SHA), producing no edge with no diagnostic — acceptable per the plan's skip-on-ambiguity rule, but only if the implementer knows to handle the FileNotFoundError from git blame.
findings.suggested_fix: In ticket 555e's plan, add: `git blame --porcelain <parent-sha> -- <path>` as the canonical invocation, with a note that a non-zero return (file absent at that SHA) triggers the skip-on-ambiguity path. This matches the existing subprocess pattern in authorship.py (explicit flags, capture_output=True, timeout=30, returncode check).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the complete `--class` enum value set (`regression`, `plan_defect`, `env_integration`, `flaky`, `preexisting`, `not_a_bug`, `duplicate`, `escalated`, `undetermined`) to the epic plan's Success Criteria or Approach section where the enum is first introduced, so the vocabulary is co-located with every AC and CLI-change description that references it.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'bug classification via a persisted close `--class` enum replacing today's validated-then-discarded free-text reason'
Plan AC4: 'the required free-text reason is replaced (not supplemented) by a bounded `--class` enum persisted on the STATUS event'
Plan Approach: 'its error message must enumerate the allowed values'

Child ticket ed13 description: 'Closed MECE vocabulary with an escape + hygiene values: `regression`, `plan_defect`, `env_integration`, `flaky`, `preexisting`, `not_a_bug`, `duplicate`, `escalated`, `undetermined`'
ABSENCE: no enum value list appears in the epic plan body, Success Criteria, Approach, or ACs
findings.finding: The `--class` enum value vocabulary is never defined anywhere in the epic plan text, yet it is referenced in Success Criteria ('a REQUIRED bounded `--class` enum'), Approach ('its error message must enumerate the allowed values'), and AC4 ('a bounded `--class` enum persisted on the STATUS event'). The vocabulary only appears in child ticket ed13's description: `regression, plan_defect, env_integration, flaky, preexisting, not_a_bug, duplicate, escalated, undetermined`. An executor implementing the epic from the plan alone cannot write the validator, the error message, or the schema without reading a child ticket — a classic T8(a) undefined-but-referenced enum gap.
findings.location: Success Criteria (bug classification bullet); AC4; Approach (back-out/expand-contract for the bug-close CLI change)
scenarios: An executor implementing transition.py `--class` validation from the epic plan alone has no vocabulary to validate against.
The error message required to 'enumerate the allowed values' cannot be written without knowing those values.
The `close_class` schema addition to ticket_state.schema.json cannot enumerate the valid strings.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria bullet that introduces `--class`, append: 'Allowed values: `regression` | `plan_defect` | `env_integration` | `flaky` | `preexisting` | `not_a_bug` | `duplicate` | `escalated` | `undetermined`.' Mirror this list in AC4's operationalization.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `ERROR` gate-sidecar outcome: specify (1) which existing sidecar event type carries it (REVIEW_RESULT or COMPLETION_VERDICT or a new type), (2) the payload fields (at minimum: `verdict: 'ERROR'`, `error_class`, `timestamp`), (3) which gate error handler(s) are the stamp sites, and (4) the interval-computation rule (ERROR→next-PASS window) — co-located with the Success Criteria bullet that introduces it.
evidence: Plan Success Criteria: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'
Decomposition Command track: 'gate-sidecar reader + `ERROR` path'
completion_sidecar.py: SCHEMA = 'completion_verifier_fail_v1', SCHEMA_PASS = 'completion_verifier_pass_v1' — only PASS/FAIL branches exist, no ERROR branch
ABSENCE: no child ticket defines the ERROR outcome field, its schema, or which gate error handler emits it
ABSENCE: no existing gate sidecar code in the repo contains an ERROR outcome path
findings.finding: The plan introduces an `ERROR` outcome on 'existing gate sidecars' as the passive instrumentation for Tier-C blindspot (a) (env/integration-diagnosis intervals), but `ERROR` as a verdict value is never defined structurally anywhere in the plan or in any child ticket. The existing REVIEW_RESULT and COMPLETION_VERDICT sidecars have `verdict` fields constrained to PASS/FAIL (verified in completion_sidecar.py SCHEMA/SCHEMA_PASS constants and the plan-review sidecar). No child ticket defines a schema amendment adding `ERROR` to the verdict enum, no gate error handler is specified as the stamp site, and no field layout for an ERROR payload is given. An executor cannot implement this instrumentation.
findings.location: Success Criteria (Tier-C blindspots, item a: 'env/integration-diagnosis via an `ERROR` outcome on the existing gate sidecars'); Decomposition (Command track: 'gate-sidecar reader + `ERROR` path')
scenarios: An executor implementing the 'gate-sidecar reader + ERROR path' in the Command track has no schema to emit or read.
The metrics reader for diagnosis intervals cannot be written without knowing which sidecar field carries ERROR, what its payload looks like, and which gate error handler stamps it.
The diagnosis interval computation (start = first ERROR stamp, end = next clean PASS) requires a start-event identity that is completely unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the complete `source` vocabulary (`structural`, `git`, `sidecar`, `snapshot`, `backfill_classified`) and the authoritative subset (`structural`, `git`, `sidecar`, `snapshot`) to AC3 or the epic Success Criteria, so the `is_authoritative()` contract is self-contained at the epic level.
evidence: Epic AC3: '`is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False and the segregation holds'
c085 description: '`source` in {structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified} and `confidence` in {high, classified}'
c085 AC3: 'a mixed input yields a series containing only `source in {structural,git,sidecar,snapshot}`'
ABSENCE: the authoritative source set is never listed in the epic plan's AC3 or Success Criteria
findings.finding: The `source` vocabulary used in AC3's `is_authoritative()` segregation test is never enumerated in the epic-level ACs or Success Criteria. AC3 states 'a `source=backfill_classified` value is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series' and asserts `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` is False, but the complementary authoritative set (what `is_authoritative()` returns True for) is only defined inside child ticket c085's description: `{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot}`. An executor implementing the segregation helper from the AC alone cannot write the authoritative allow-list.
findings.location: AC3; child ticket c085 approach section
scenarios: An executor writing `is_authoritative()` from the epic AC alone knows only that `backfill_classified` returns False, not what positive values return True — leading to an overly permissive or overly restrictive implementation.
A new source value added later (e.g. 'ci_harvest') has no defined position in the authoritative set, so it silently falls to the wrong bucket.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC3: 'The authoritative source set is `{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot}`; `is_authoritative(s)` returns True iff `s` is in this set. The full `source` vocabulary is `{structural, git, sidecar, snapshot, backfill_classified}`; `confidence` is `{high, classified}`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit deps `0b96` and `555e` to child 2f8e's ticket metadata, and add 2f8e to the parent's cross-ticket coordination ordering note.
evidence: 2f8e Approach: 'reuse `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`' — but blame.py is created by 555e, not 2f8e
2f8e draws `caused_by` links — the `caused_by` relation is created by 0b96
Parent cross-ticket coordination section does not list 2f8e's deps on 0b96 or 555e explicitly
If 2f8e is run before 555e, `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py` does not exist; if before 0b96, `caused_by` is not a valid relation
findings.finding: Child 2f8e has undeclared dependencies on both 0b96 (the `caused_by` relation must exist before links can be drawn) and 555e (the `blame.py` module is created by 555e and reused by 2f8e). These dependencies are not listed in 2f8e and would cause 2f8e to fail if attempted before those children land.
findings.location: child 2f8e-0f7e-6fea-4825, Approach: 'Reuse the same conservative single-culprit resolver as the live auto-derivation... reuse `src/rebar/metrics/blame.py`'
scenarios: A developer attempts to land and run 2f8e before 555e; the import of blame.py fails at runtime
The backfill script runs in CI before 0b96's relation is merged; rebar rejects caused_by as an invalid relation value
findings.suggested_fix: In 2f8e's ticket, declare blocking dependencies on 0b96 (caused_by relation) and 555e (blame.py). Add to the parent's coordination section: 'land 0b96 and 555e before 2f8e'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 8bc5 as an explicit dependency of 3c07, and add an integration-level test (or shared schema fixture) asserting that 3c07's ERROR reader accepts exactly the payload shape that 8bc5's writer produces.
evidence: 3c07 Approach references ERROR outcome without citing 8bc5 as the definer
8bc5 defines the sidecar payload: `error{cause,evidence_ref}` and `verdict=ERROR`
3c07 AC1: 'an ERROR row paired with a later PASS on the same ticket yields a diagnosis-interval duration equal to the fixture timestamp delta'
3c07 does not list 8bc5 as a dependency; the schema coupling is implicit
findings.finding: Child 3c07 (gate-sidecar reader) has an undeclared dependency on 8bc5 for the ERROR verdict format and the `error{cause,evidence_ref}` object schema. 3c07's AC mentions pairing ERROR rows with PASS rows and computing diagnosis intervals, but the ERROR verdict is defined and written only by 8bc5. If 8bc5 changes the error object shape (e.g., renames `cause` to `error_type`), 3c07 silently breaks.
findings.location: child 3c07-27ac-4b61-47a3, Approach: 'env-diagnosis pairs each ERROR with the next PASS on the same ticket'; child 8bc5 defines `error{cause,evidence_ref}`
scenarios: 8bc5 is modified post-landing to rename the error object fields; 3c07's reader breaks at runtime but no compile-time or test-time coupling catches it
A developer writes 3c07's fixture with a different ERROR payload shape than 8bc5 produces; the integration never works end-to-end
findings.suggested_fix: Define the ERROR sidecar payload schema in a shared location (e.g., `src/rebar/llm/completion_sidecar.py` schema constants) and have both 8bc5 and 3c07 import from it, so a field rename is caught at import time.

findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Net process friction does not increase' has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion that observably validates it — there is no before/after comparison, user-study proxy, or measurable baseline to confirm the criterion was met.
findings.location: Success Criteria – 'Net process friction does not increase'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC such as: 'Bug-close command requires ≤ N keystrokes/flags compared to the prior free-text flow, verified by a side-by-side CLI invocation comparison in the test suite or documented in a decision record.'

findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Net process friction does not increase' is subjective and has no defined measurement method or baseline, making it unevaluable. None of the four ACs provide a concrete check for this claim beyond AC4's field-existence assertion.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Net process friction does not increase'
findings.suggested_fix: State the current required-argument count for `rebar close` (e.g., 'currently requires 1 positional arg + 1 `--reason` free-text; after this change requires 1 positional arg + 1 `--class` enum with no free-text') so friction is concretely comparable.

findings.finding: The Success Criterion 'Historical data is backfilled wherever a durable source exists; backfilled/classified values are labeled with source+confidence and segregated from authoritative structural signals' has no dedicated Acceptance Criterion to verify the backfill was actually executed and correctly labeled.
findings.location: Success Criteria – final bullet ('Historical data is backfilled…')

findings.finding: The bug-close CLI change (replacing the required free-text reason with a `--class` enum) is a breaking interface change for any agent or script that currently passes the free-text argument, but the plan provides no rollback/back-out path or expand-contract sequencing for the CLI surface — it only asserts that old *records* remain valid, which does not address the broken call sites.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4; Decomposition — Instrumentation track; Approach paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a deprecation window: accept both `--reason <text>` (mapped to `class=unclassified`, logged as deprecated) and `--class <enum>` for one release cycle, then remove `--reason`. Document the enum extension path (e.g., additive enum values are non-breaking; removal requires a new deprecation cycle). State the rollback step if the enum set ships incomplete: revert to accepting free-text while a corrected enum is designed.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `git blame`-based auto-derivation of `caused_by` links as a non-optional Tier-C instrumentation deliverable, but `git blame` is never used anywhere in the codebase and the specific invocation surface is entirely unspecified: which file(s) and line(s) to blame for a given bug ticket, how to handle multi-file bugs, merge commits, renames (`--follow`), or tickets with no `file_impact`. The capability gap — mapping a bug ticket to a blameworthy commit — is non-trivial and unverified. A naive `git blame` on the first `file_impact` file at HEAD would silently produce wrong provenance links for refactors, rebases, and bugs with no file_impact.
findings.location: Instrumentation track / Decomposition: 'blame-based `caused_by` auto-derivation + `--caused-by` param + bug-template advisory'

findings.finding: The `caused_by` auto-derivation via `git blame` is mechanically ambiguous: running blame on the post-fix state attributes lines to the fixing commit, not the introducing commit. To identify what caused the bug, blame must target the pre-fix state (e.g. the parent of the fixing commit, or the file at the commit just before the fix). The plan says 'auto-derived from blame' without specifying which state is blamed, so an implementation that naively blames the current file will systematically return the wrong commit.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, Tier-C blindspots bullet: 'bug provenance via a new caused_by link relation (auto-derived from blame, plus an optional param)'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify: run `git blame <fix_commit>^1 -- <changed_file>` (the parent of the fixing commit) on the lines that the fix deleted/modified, to identify the commit that last wrote the buggy code. The `--caused-by` optional param remains the override for cases where blame is ambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the bug-close friction AC to explicitly verify *removal* of `--reason` from the required arguments list — e.g., add '`rebar transition --help` output does NOT contain `--reason` as a required argument' or assert `--reason` is absent/optional — so the AC covers both sides of the swap as the rubric requires for migrations.
evidence: AC: 'a `rebar transition --help` diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments'
Success Criteria: 'the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none'
'For migrations, criteria verify BOTH removal and replacement' rubric requirement
AC verifies `.close_class` exists in JSON output (replacement) but no AC line asserts `--reason` is absent from the required-args list (removal)
findings.finding: The bug-close friction AC verifies replacement via 'no net increase in required bug-close arguments,' but this condition is satisfiable without actually removing `--reason` (e.g., `--reason` becomes optional while `--class` is added as required — net count stays flat). The AC does not explicitly assert that `--reason` is removed or made non-required, leaving a verification loophole for the migration.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — fourth bullet ('Bug-close friction stays flat'); Success Criteria — fourth bullet (migration description)
scenarios: Implementation makes `--reason` optional (not removed) and adds `--class` as required; `rebar transition --help` shows same required-arg count; AC passes; but `--reason` still appears in the help and old callers silently succeed with the old flag rather than failing loudly as intended.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the fourth AC bullet: 'AND `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --class <value>` succeeds without `--reason`, while `rebar transition <bug> in_progress closed --reason <value>` (without `--class`) fails with an actionable error listing the `--class` enum values — confirming removal, not merely non-supplementation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to ed13 asserting that `rebar transition --help` output for bug-close shows exactly the same number of required arguments as the pre-epic baseline (swap verified, not supplement), and that a non-bug ticket close does not require `--class`.
evidence: Parent AC4: 'verified by diffing `rebar transition --help` against the pre-epic baseline (the change swaps one required arg for another, adds none)'
ed13 AC1: tests --class persists; AC2: tests no --class fails; AC3: invalid rejected; AC4: undetermined accepted
No ed13 AC mentions asserting --reason is removed or testing the net required-argument count
Parent AC4 also says 'No non-bug transition gains a new required argument' — no child tests this invariant for non-bug ticket types
findings.finding: No child explicitly tests that the `rebar transition --help` diff shows no net increase in required bug-close arguments (i.e., that `--reason` is removed, not just supplemented by `--class`). The parent AC4 operationalizes 'friction stays flat' via a help-diff assertion, but ed13's ACs only test that `--class` is required and valid/invalid values work — they do not assert that `--reason` is no longer accepted or that the total required-argument count has not increased.
findings.location: child ed13-e224-1ece-44ff, Acceptance Criteria; parent AC4
scenarios: ed13 implements --class as REQUIRED and also keeps --reason as required (accidentally supplementing instead of replacing); all ed13 ACs pass but the parent's friction-flat guarantee is violated
A non-bug ticket type gains a required argument due to a code-path mistake; no test catches it
findings.suggested_fix: In `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_bug_close_class.py`, add a test that (a) runs `rebar transition --help` and asserts `--reason` is not listed as required for bug closes, and (b) closes a non-bug ticket without `--class` and asserts exit 0.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `--batch-size` / `--sleep-ms` option to `backfill_caused_by.py` and document that the script is safe to kill-and-resume (the skip-already-linked check acts as the checkpoint), so the AC in 2f8e is explicit about resumability under interruption.
evidence: 2f8e description: 'iterate closed bugs, reuse src/rebar/metrics/blame.py, draw caused_by on high-confidence single culprits' — no mention of batch size, sleep/throttle, or progress checkpointing
T11(b): 'Large backfills are batched/throttled, not one giant transaction'
findings.finding: The backfill_caused_by.py script runs git-blame over every closed bug in the store but specifies no batching or throttling strategy. On a large store, this could be a long-running single-shot operation with no pause/resume between bugs.
findings.location: Decomposition > Backfill track; ticket 2f8e (scripts/backfill_caused_by.py)
scenarios: A repo with hundreds of closed bugs triggers hundreds of git-blame subprocesses in a single unbounded loop, exhausting disk I/O or hitting git index contention under concurrent CI activity.

A partially-completed run that times out or is killed cannot be resumed from where it stopped without re-scanning all already-processed bugs (idempotency via skip is present, but re-scanning the full list from the start is still O(n) re-work with no checkpoint).
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/backfill_caused_by.py`, process closed bugs in configurable chunks (e.g. `--batch-size 50`, default 50) with a brief `time.sleep` between batches, and emit a progress line per batch. Because the skip-already-linked check makes re-runs idempotent, kill-and-restart already works — just document it explicitly in the ticket AC and the script's `--help`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the GitHub Actions coverage harvest adapter's precondition is satisfied by adding a parseable coverage artifact upload (e.g., `--cov-report json` + `actions/upload-artifact`) to CI, OR explicitly document that this adapter starts from the epic's landing date rather than backfilling historical runs, and remove the implied backfill claim for coverage.
evidence: .github/workflows/test.yml line 393: `--cov=rebar --cov-report=term-missing:skip-covered` — no JSON/XML report, no upload-artifact step for coverage data
.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml line 386: identical pattern, no parseable coverage artifact
No `actions/upload-artifact` step for coverage in test.yml or gerrit-verify.yaml
GitHub Actions REST API does not expose per-run coverage numbers as a first-class endpoint — coverage data must be in an uploaded artifact to be harvestable
Evidence class: Contradicted
findings.finding: The GitHub Actions coverage harvest adapter depends on structured, machine-readable per-run coverage data being accessible from CI, but the current CI produces only a human-readable terminal report (--cov-report=term-missing:skip-covered) with no artifact upload. GitHub Actions has no native API endpoint that surfaces per-run coverage percentages; harvesting historical coverage snapshots requires either a JSON/XML coverage artifact upload in each workflow run (not currently configured) or a third-party integration. The precondition for this adapter is Contradicted by the current CI setup.
findings.location: Backfill track — 'GitHub-Actions coverage + CI-health harvest (optional adapter)'
scenarios: Executor implements the GitHub Actions coverage harvest adapter and discovers there are no historical coverage artifacts to harvest, because the CI never produced parseable ones.
Backfill of complexity/coverage snapshots silently produces zero rows because the adapter has nothing to read from the Actions API.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--cov-report json:coverage.json` alongside the existing `term-missing` report and an `actions/upload-artifact` step (scoped to a named artifact like `coverage-report`) in test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml. The harvest adapter then fetches artifact list via `gh api repos/{owner}/{repo}/actions/artifacts?name=coverage-report` and downloads each run's JSON. Alternatively, explicitly scope the coverage backfill to 'going-forward only' in the plan and remove it from the backfill track.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
